court: Supreme Court of the United States
author: Chief Justice John G. Roberts Jr.
issue: Whether the Government's acquisition of historical cell-site location information (CSLI) from wireless carriers constitutes a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant.
holding: The Government's acquisition of seven days or more of historical cell-site records is a Fourth Amendment search; police generally must obtain a warrant supported by probable cause.
facts_summary: Timothy Carpenter and Timothy Sanders were charged with nine armed robberies in Michigan and Ohio. FBI agents obtained 127 days of historical cell-site location information (CSLI) for Carpenter's mobile phone from MetroPCS and Sprint, pursuant to court orders under the Stored Communications Act (18 U.S.C. § 2703(d)) — a standard below probable cause. The records placed Carpenter's phone at robbery locations during the crimes. Carpenter moved to suppress the CSLI, arguing a warrant was required. The District Court and Sixth Circuit denied suppression.
procedural_history: United States v. Carpenter, 819 F.3d 880 (6th Cir. 2016) — affirmed denial of suppression

key_precedents.principle: Fourth Amendment protects people, not places; reasonable expectation of privacy test.
GPS tracking of vehicle for 28 days is a search; trespass and privacy theories.
Warrant required to search digital contents of cell phone incident to arrest.
Pen register / numbers dialed — third-party doctrine; limited by Carpenter.
Bank records — no reasonable expectation of privacy in information voluntarily conveyed to third parties.

statutes.title: Stored Communications Act — Required disclosure of customer communications or records

demo_scenario.research_question: Did the FBI's warrantless acquisition of 127 days of historical cell-site location records violate the Fourth Amendment?
demo_scenario.jurisdiction: United States Supreme Court (on certiorari from Sixth Circuit)
demo_scenario.relief_sought: Suppression of CSLI evidence; reversal of convictions
